original_abstract:
Single-pixel imaging allows for the capture of scenes without a direct line of sight to the object, yet producing high-quality images remains difficult, particularly when faced with noisy ambient lighting. In this paper, we introduce a single-pixel imaging method that achieves high reconstruction fidelity by directly acquiring the object’s Fourier spectrum. Our approach employs phase-shifted sinusoidal structured light to sample the spectrum, which is then transformed into the spatial domain via an inverse Fourier transform to reveal the final image. 

Because this technique does not require a direct view of the scene, it offers significant flexibility in detector placement; sensors can be positioned anywhere they can intercept scattered light signals. Furthermore, by leveraging the inherent sparsity of natural images in the Fourier domain, the method functions as a compressive sampling framework, enabling accurate image reconstruction from sub-Nyquist measurements. We experimentally demonstrate the robustness of this system by successfully capturing clear images using a hidden detector, even in environments with heavy background illumination noise.
humanized_abstract:
Single-pixel imaging enables the capture of a scene from an oblique viewpoint off of the line of sight to the object of interest, but achieving high quality remains a major challenge, especially in the presence of cluttered background ambient light. This paper presents a novel single-pixel imaging approach that is capable of achieving high image quality. The method instantaneously obtains the Fourier spectrum of the object of interest and, via a simple inverse Fourier transform, obtains the desired image. Our approach uses phase-shifted sinusoidal structured light to modulate the spectrum of the object light that reaches the single-pixel detector.

Our approach offers flexibility with respect to where the detector is placed: unlike competing methods, it does not require a direct view to the object of interest, and instead scatters of light can be detected from anywhere. Because natural scenes are sparse in the Fourier domain, it can also be viewed as a form of compressive sampling and the object of interest can be reliably recovered from few below Nyquist sampling rate measurements. Experimental demonstrations showing that it is possible to obtain very high resolution hidden images using the approach, even in strong background illumination noise, are provided.

document.output:
Single-pixel imaging enables the capture of a scene from an oblique viewpoint off of the line of sight to the object of interest, but achieving high quality remains a major challenge, especially in the presence of cluttered background ambient light. This paper presents a novel single-pixel imaging approach that is capable of achieving high image quality. The method instantaneously obtains the Fourier spectrum of the object of interest and, via a simple inverse Fourier transform, obtains the desired image. Our approach uses phase-shifted sinusoidal structured light to modulate the spectrum of the object light that reaches the single-pixel detector.

Our approach offers flexibility with respect to where the detector is placed: unlike competing methods, it does not require a direct view to the object of interest, and instead scatters of light can be detected from anywhere. Because natural scenes are sparse in the Fourier domain, it can also be viewed as a form of compressive sampling and the object of interest can be reliably recovered from few below Nyquist sampling rate measurements. Experimental demonstrations showing that it is possible to obtain very high resolution hidden images using the approach, even in strong background illumination noise, are provided.
document.input:
Single-pixel imaging allows for the capture of scenes without a direct line of sight to the object, yet producing high-quality images remains difficult, particularly when faced with noisy ambient lighting. In this paper, we introduce a single-pixel imaging method that achieves high reconstruction fidelity by directly acquiring the object’s Fourier spectrum. Our approach employs phase-shifted sinusoidal structured light to sample the spectrum, which is then transformed into the spatial domain via an inverse Fourier transform to reveal the final image. 

Because this technique does not require a direct view of the scene, it offers significant flexibility in detector placement; sensors can be positioned anywhere they can intercept scattered light signals. Furthermore, by leveraging the inherent sparsity of natural images in the Fourier domain, the method functions as a compressive sampling framework, enabling accurate image reconstruction from sub-Nyquist measurements. We experimentally demonstrate the robustness of this system by successfully capturing clear images using a hidden detector, even in environments with heavy background illumination noise.
